\subsection{Execution Quality}\label{sec:eval_task_execution}

Execution-quality metrics ask whether a policy not only completes a task, but does so in a stable, coherent, and physically acceptable manner. Binary success rates are too coarse for this purpose: they cannot distinguish early catastrophic failure from near-complete but unstable execution, especially in long-horizon and bimanual settings. Recent work therefore evaluates execution quality along three main families of metrics: task completion and progress, trajectory quality, and coordination quality.

For long-horizon tasks, stage-wise progress metrics provide a more informative view than a single terminal success label. RoboEval~\cite{wang2025roboeval} decomposes manipulation into sequential stages and measures progress as the fraction completed, while REALM~\cite{sedlacek2025realm} extends this idea with Task Progression, a continuous score that weights stages by importance and quantifies robustness under perturbations through Root Mean Square Deviation.

Execution quality is also reflected in how a trajectory is produced. Smoothness metrics such as SPARC~\cite{balasubramanian2015sparc} and LDLJ~\cite{antonelli2023movement} quantify jerk and motion regularity in frequency and time domains, while Dynamic Time Warping measures how closely a policy trajectory follows expert demonstrations under temporal misalignment. For bimanual manipulation, quality further depends on coordination between limbs: RoboEval measures this through velocity divergence and height discrepancy between end-effectors. Taken together, these metrics diagnose execution much more precisely than binary success alone, but they still capture only part of the picture, since subjective acceptability and broader transfer behavior require separate evaluation protocols.

\subsection{Data Efficiency and Data Quality}\label{sec:eval_data_sample}

Data-efficiency and data-quality metrics ask how effectively a system converts demonstrations into capability, and which parts of a dataset are actually responsible for that capability. This axis matters because embodied manipulation is often constrained by the cost, heterogeneity, and uneven quality of collected data. Current practice therefore evaluates three related aspects: sample efficiency, dataset quality, and per-sample utility.

Sample efficiency is usually summarized through learning curves rather than single end-point scores. A common summary statistic is the Area Under the Learning Curve (AULC), which integrates success rate over the number of demonstrations:
\begin{equation}
  \text{AULC} = \int_0^{N_{\max}} SR(n)\, dn \approx \sum_{k} SR(n_k) \cdot \Delta n_k
\end{equation}
where $SR(n)$ is the success rate after training on $n$ demonstrations. Higher AULC indicates that useful capability is obtained with fewer samples, making it more informative than reporting final success after a fixed data budget.

Dataset quality is increasingly measured through coverage- or information-based criteria. DemInf~\cite{hejna2025robot}, for example, frames dataset quality in terms of the marginal contribution of demonstrations to state-action coverage. A complementary family of methods scores individual samples by their downstream training utility. CUPID~\cite{agia2025cupidcuratingdatarobot} represents this direction through influence-based valuation, estimating which demonstrations improve validation behavior and which act as harmful outliers. These metrics connect naturally to the curation methods discussed in \S\ref{sec:data_refinement}, but they still do not establish whether a dataset supports robust transfer beyond its training distribution. That question belongs to the next axis: generalization and transfer validity.

\subsection{Generalization and Transfer Validity}\label{sec:eval_benchmarking}

Generalization and transfer metrics ask whether capability persists beyond the training distribution and whether intermediate evaluation proxies predict real-world behavior. This axis therefore combines three related concerns: robustness to distribution shift, evaluation protocols for judging policy quality, and transfer validity across proxies such as simulators and learned world models.

Robustness to distribution shift is typically measured through performance gaps between in-distribution and perturbed settings. REALM~\cite{sedlacek2025realm}, for example, organizes perturbations into visual, semantic, and physical categories and reports degradation through metrics such as the OOD success-rate gap and Root Mean Square Deviation. These metrics capture whether a policy remains reliable when objects, scenes, or interaction conditions change.

Protocol design matters as much as the metric itself. RoboArena~\cite{atreya2025roboarena} uses pairwise human comparison to infer global skill ratings in a distributed real-world benchmark, while AutoEval~\cite{zhou2025autoeval} replaces manual annotation with VLM-based success assessment. These protocols are useful because execution quality is not always reducible to a single scripted success detector.

Transfer validity asks whether conclusions drawn from scalable proxies remain predictive of physical deployment. SIMPLER~\cite{li2025simpler} studies this question for hand-crafted simulators through measures such as Mismatched Ranking Violations, which quantify when policy rankings disagree between simulation and reality. WorldArena~\cite{shang2026worldarena} extends the same question to learned world models by evaluating whether they function reliably as policy evaluators or planning environments. Together, these metrics and protocols make generalization more measurable, but they still stop short of the operational costs that appear during sustained deployment.

\subsection{Deployment Efficiency and Resource Cost}\label{sec:eval_energy}

Deployment-cost metrics ask what capability costs once a policy leaves the benchmark setting and enters sustained operation. In principle this axis includes latency, memory footprint, and hardware load, but current embodied-manipulation evaluation is still dominated by energy-oriented measures. This emphasis is reasonable because energy consumption directly affects operating cost and deployment feasibility in long-running industrial and domestic settings.

The first family of metrics decomposes where energy is consumed. ECDP~\cite{heredia2023ecdp} separates total power into electronics standby power, mechanical friction, copper loss, and useful mechanical work, showing that control electronics often dominate total consumption. This matters because it shifts attention from trajectory optimization alone toward hardware-software co-design.

A second family normalizes efficiency so that systems can be compared across platforms and operating conditions. Metrics in this category include energy-utilization ratios~\cite{vidussi2021energy,heredia2025evaluating} and the Cost of Transport, which normalizes energy by mass and distance. A third line treats deployment cost as a multi-objective trade-off: surrogate-based optimization~\cite{rezali2025lstm} maps trajectory parameters to energy, execution time, and jerk, enabling Pareto analysis across competing goals.

Compared with task or transfer evaluation, this axis remains less standardized. Energy is increasingly measured, but latency, runtime reliability, and compute footprint are still rarely reported in a comparable way. For embodied manipulation systems, deployment cost therefore remains an important but only partially quantified part of evaluation.

The evaluation methodologies above reveal a fundamental tension: as manipulation systems scale in complexity, evaluation itself becomes a bottleneck. Binary success rates obscure failure modes, data-value metrics do not by themselves establish transfer, and simulation or world-model proxies remain imperfect predictors of real-world behavior. More broadly, current practice remains fragmented across execution quality, data value, transfer validity, and deployment cost, with no unified framework for trading off these objectives. The final section examines these persistent challenges alongside broader limitations in representation, deployment, and ecosystem infrastructure.

\section{Open Challenges and Future Directions}\label{sec:challenges}

The preceding sections suggest that the main open problems in embodied manipulation no longer lie within isolated modules alone. They arise at the interfaces among embodiment, data, learning, deployment, and evaluation. A planner may be semantically capable but physically under-grounded; a policy may score well offline but fail under runtime latency or contact uncertainty; a data pipeline may scale, yet still omit the failure and recovery regimes needed for robust deployment. We therefore group the remaining challenges into three cross-layer themes: physical grounding of representation and control, reliable deployment under real-world constraints, and shared infrastructure for data and evaluation.

\subsection{Physical Grounding and Transfer}\label{sec:representation_reasoning}

The first open challenge is to align high-level representation with the physical structure of manipulation. Current systems can often parse instructions, generate plans, and imitate actions, but they still struggle when success depends on contact state, force exchange, hidden object dynamics, or embodiment-specific constraints. This gap appears repeatedly across the survey: in end-effectors and contact sensing (\S\ref{sec:embodiment_hardware}), in the limits of current data support (\S\ref{sec:embodiment_dataset}), and in the divide between cognitive planning and policy execution (\S\ref{sec:embodiment_method}). More data and larger models help, but they do not by themselves guarantee that semantic competence will translate into physically reliable behavior.

Progress here will likely require tighter grounding at three levels. First, representations must integrate vision with proprioception and tactile feedback so that contact becomes observable rather than inferred indirectly from images alone. Second, planning and policy learning must become more transferable across embodiments without ignoring morphology: a useful representation cannot be so robot-specific that transfer fails, nor so abstract that it loses kinematic and dynamic meaning. Third, continual adaptation must preserve both physical skill and general reasoning. At present, many systems still trade off dexterity against semantic breadth, especially after narrow embodiment-specific fine-tuning. A central research direction is therefore to build representations that remain physically grounded, transferable across platforms, and stable under ongoing adaptation.

\subsection{Reliable Deployment and Runtime Safety}\label{sec:deployment_safety}

The second challenge is to close the gap between benchmark success and sustained deployment. Manipulation policies are increasingly evaluated in realistic settings, but real-world operation still exposes failure modes that are easy to miss in offline training or short-horizon testing. Latency, sensing dropouts, contact shocks, out-of-distribution states, and action-serving bottlenecks all become first-order concerns once a policy must run continuously on hardware. As discussed in \S\ref{sec:deployment_pipeline} and \S\ref{sec:evaluation}, deployment quality is not captured by task success alone; it also depends on stability, recovery behavior, runtime efficiency, and resource cost.

Future progress will likely depend on treating deployment as a primary design objective rather than a final implementation step. This includes policies that degrade gracefully under uncertainty, runtime monitors that can detect anomaly or loss of controllability before catastrophic failure, and deployment stacks that explicitly balance latency, compute, and energy against control quality. Safety should also be understood more broadly than collision avoidance alone: in manipulation, safe behavior depends on force regulation, fallback behavior under partial failure, and the ability to recover from unexpected contact without destabilizing the full system. More generally, embodied models need deployment pathways that are staged, measurable, and reversible, so that adaptation on hardware does not come at the cost of brittleness or loss of prior capability.

\subsection{Data and Evaluation Infrastructure}\label{sec:ecosystem_infrastructure}

The third challenge is infrastructural. Even when methods improve, progress remains difficult to accumulate if data formats, embodiment interfaces, and evaluation protocols are inconsistent across laboratories and platforms. This issue appears throughout the data ecosystem in \S\ref{sec:embodiment_dataset}: real-world corpora differ in calibration, sensing, action representation, and failure annotation; simulation stacks expose different physical assumptions and task abstractions; and large pipelines still vary in how they align synthetic and real data. As a result, much of the field still depends on bespoke preprocessing, ad hoc retargeting, and limited cross-paper comparability.

Two infrastructure gaps are especially important. The first is the lack of standardized data support for difficult regimes: failures, recoveries, contact transitions, cross-embodiment demonstrations, and long-horizon maintenance of world state remain much less available than nominal success trajectories. The second is the lack of calibrated evaluation infrastructure. Simulators, real-world benchmarks, and learned world models are all useful, but their relationships are not yet systematically characterized enough to support reliable substitution or ranking transfer. A mature ecosystem will therefore require not only larger corpora, but also shared schemas, reusable pipelines, stronger metadata about embodiment and sensing, and evaluation protocols that jointly report execution quality, data efficiency, transfer validity, and deployment cost. Without this infrastructure, progress will remain difficult to reproduce and even harder to compare.

\section{Conclusion}

This survey has analyzed embodied manipulation intelligence through the organizational chain of \emph{embodiment $\to$ data $\to$ learning $\to$ evaluation}. We showed that hardware constraints---end-effector morphology, platform kinematics, middleware latency---propagate directly into data design, which in turn shapes what learning paradigms can achieve and how deployment must be engineered. The three-tier dataset taxonomy provides a principled basis for comparing data investments; the cognitive planning / policy learning dual pathway with Type~I/II/III integration taxonomy supersedes the monolithic VLA narrative by revealing structural trade-offs between semantic generality and physical precision; and the three-stage deployment lifecycle (offline/online/runtime) formalizes the engineering constraints that bridge laboratory models to real-world operation. The open challenges identified in \S\ref{sec:challenges}---representation bottlenecks, deployment safety, and ecosystem fragmentation---arise precisely at the interfaces between these stages, suggesting that future progress depends less on improving individual components than on tightening the coupling across the entire chain.
